sessions.title: Registration & Check-In
Flutter is everywhere
sessions.description:
As Flutter continues to expand its reach—powering everything from automotive multimedia systems to the next generation of agentic applications—the framework is evolving to meet the needs of a global, distributed community and ecosystem.

Beyond the code, we will explore the critical role of community-driven governance in sustaining Flutter's growth. With Canonical now serving as a lead maintainer for multi-window desktop support, we will discuss how Flutter is shifting toward a more decentralized governance model to ensure the framework remains "built by everyone, for everyone". Join us to see how we are reimagining the developer experience for an AI-driven world through Agentic Hot Reload and GenUI, and learn how you can contribute to the roadmap that will shape the future of cross-platform development.

sessions.title: Break
Flutter at Scale: A Roadmap for the Modern Enterprise
sessions.description: With countless companies across every industry running Flutter in production, the conversation has shifted from “What can Flutter do?” to “How do we maximize its impact at scale?” In this opening keynote, Flutter Outbound PM Abdallah Shaban and VGV CEO David DeRemer explore Flutter’s next chapter. They will dive into how Flutter and the broader ecosystem of contributors are investing to meet enterprise demands, and the emerging market patterns VGV sees as teams scale their Flutter footprint.

sessions.title: Break
Flutter Migration at Scale: Migrate Tamara App in 10 Weeks Journey
sessions.description:
What does it really take to migrate a large-scale, Fintech, and used by millions production app to Flutter—fast, safely, and without breaking the business or user trust?

In this talk, I'm gonna share the behind-the-scenes story of how the Tamara app was migrated from native to Flutter in just 10 weeks. This isn’t a “hello world” migration—this is real-world engineering under pressure: undocumented business logic buried in Jira tickets, teams with mixed backgrounds, and the constant challenge of moving fast without things catching fire.

From convincing stakeholders to trust the move, to onboarding engineers and building internal tooling powered by AI, this session dives deep into the practical decisions, trade-offs, and battle-tested strategies that made the migration possible.

If you’re thinking about Flutter at scale—or already in the middle of the chaos—this talk will give you a realistic playbook (with a few scars and lessons along the way).

sessions.title: Break
From Vibe-Coded Viral MVP to Launchable Product in 90 Days
sessions.description:
What happens when a viral app built with AI-generated code needs to become a real, shippable product fast?

This is the story of Divine, a groundbreaking short-form video platform and a reboot of the original Vine — the iconic app acquired by Twitter — now reimagined for a decentralized world and backed by Jack Dorsey. Its CEO had built an exciting MVP using vibe coding, but turning that into a stable, production-grade Flutter app was a different challenge entirely. That's where Very Good Ventures came in.

In this session, the Divine and the VGV team will share the inside story of how we went from a codebase with 500+ open issues and no CI/CD infrastructure to a live product on Zapstore — in 90 days. We'll walk through the strategic decisions, the engineering methodology, and the human side of running a high-velocity product engagement in the age of AI.

Key Takeaways:
- From vibe-coded to production cadence: assessing and stabilizing AI-generated codebases: We'll share the assessment framework VGV used to triage 400+ test cases and classify what was working, partially working, broken, and not yet built, giving founders and engineering leads a replicable model for inherited or AI-generated codebases. We'll also walk through how a "Stability-First" roadmap helped align a fast-moving founder with an engineering team focused on long-term quality, and how daily transparency built the trust needed to make hard trade-offs fast.

- Agentic Flutter Engineering in practice: Learn how VGV embedded AI agents (including Claude Code) as active co-developers and code reviewers throughout the entire SDLC as a structured methodology that compressed delivery timelines without sacrificing architectural integrity.

- Building on NOSTR: Flutter meets decentralized social: Divine is built on NOSTR, an open, decentralized protocol for social media that is rapidly gaining traction as an alternative to centralized platforms. Its event-driven, cryptographic architecture requires a different mental model than traditional REST or GraphQL APIs. We'll share how VGV leveraged existing NOSTR knowledge within the team to integrate it cleanly within Flutter, and why decentralized protocols are becoming an increasingly valuable skill for mobile engineers building the next generation of social apps.

- What vibe coding means for the Flutter ecosystem: A candid conversation between a founder who built with it and an engineering firm that inherited it: the opportunities, the risks, and what comes next as AI-generated Flutter apps become more common.

sessions.title: Lunch
The Flutter Enterprise Journey: From Native Apps to AI-Enabled Multi-Platform
sessions.description:
This session explores how enterprise teams are navigating the strategic shift from native mobile development to Flutter's unified multi-platform approach, examining the real-world challenges, trade-offs, and wins along the way. We'll dive into why Flutter is uniquely positioned as an AI-era framework - where a single shared codebase dramatically reduces the token overhead of AI-assisted development, meaning fewer redundant prompts, less repeated logic across platforms, and faster iteration cycles when working alongside tools like GitHub Copilot or Claude.

Attendees will walk away with a practical roadmap for modernizing their enterprise app strategy with AI at the center, unlocking compounding efficiency gains across iOS, Android, web, and desktop without sacrificing the performance, security, and maintainability that production environments demand.

sessions.title: Break
Flutter on Big Screen & Beyond: Building Embedder for LG webOS
sessions.description:
- Reannounce official Flutter webOS SDK Release on 6/30/2026 (Release embargo requested until May 19-20, Google I/O 2026)
- Technical Guide on Flutter webOS SDK 
- Embedder, NDK, Docker, etc.
- Flutter webOS Plugins for ASTC, DRM (Widevine, PlayReady), Soloud, Gamepads, etc.
- Future plan for Flutter in webOS, including AI integration, Rendering/Memory improvement, and platform expansion

sessions.title: Break
Why SoFi ♥️Flutter
sessions.description:
Several years ago, after some initial explorations SoFi undertook the work to migrate from doing native development on Android and iOS to doing cross-platform development with Flutter. Since then our products, our customer base, and our team have grown tremendously. We’ll cover the technical and operational benefits we’ve seen from using Flutter as well as challenges and lessons learned along the way.

The summary of the talk's content includes:
- Cross-Platform Excellence: Flutter enables true cross-platform development with minimal duplication of work, allowing simultaneous feature launches on iOS and Android. The presentation will discuss how SoFi's monorepo is now 98.6% Dart, reducing the need for native code, and will cover internal tools like somo and the design system catalog app for approachable macOS development.

- Reliability and Efficiency: SoFi experiences high reliability with a stable crash rate (over 99.95% crash-free sessions). Development cycles are tight due to hot code reloading and fast build times.

- Customization and Interoperability: The talk will delve into the deep customization achieved through design system work, including the decision to move away from Material, and the ability to look at underlying source code. Other topics include easy learning and leveraging native SDKs via native interop.

sessions.title: Break
Flutter at Enterprise scale at GEICO
sessions.description:
At GEICO, we've been working towards transitioning our Frontend experience to be as platform agnostic as possible for ~2 years. A lot of things have changed since we began and we'd like to showcase some of the architectural strategies/tooling/ai workflows we've adopted to help us accelerate the transition moving forward.

1. Discuss some challenges that come from adopting Flutter at GEICO
  a. Different organizational structures between mobile/web
  b. Need to ship in increments and often (interleaving old and new)
  c. Need for production ready capabilities as fast as possible
  d. Security tools don't support Flutter (web) well
  e. Observability differences betweeen platforms (OpenTel, etc)
2. Discuss our Migration strategy/architecture
3. How Flutter is doing in production (web/ios/android)
4. Discuss AI integration with workflows and managing organizational context

sessions.title: Break
Conquering chaos at scale
sessions.description:
When Fluter apps are used by 10s of millions of users, your projects and codebase look a little different. You’re dealing with expanding feature lists, growing teams, and operational challenges. 

In this panel, we’ll dive into the trenches of delivering Flutter at an enterprise scale. Industry experts from Sofi and eBay will share how they architect and manage their CI/CD pipelines, their go-to tools, and how they’re using AI to move faster. 

Whether you’re a seasoned Flutter team or just starting to spread your wings you’ll walk away with actionable insights to scale your Flutter app.

sessions.title: Party
Making AI Predictable: Skill-Driven Flutter Development
sessions.description:
AI coding has evolved beyond one-off prompts. Modern IDEs now support structured, reusable skills — persistent behaviors that shape how your tools think, generate, and refactor code.

In this talk, we’ll explore what skills actually are, how they differ from traditional prompting, and how to integrate them into your Flutter development workflow. You’ll learn how to design skill files, structure them effectively, and use them to standardize architecture, enforce patterns, and accelerate real-world Flutter projects.

By the end, you’ll understand how to move from prompts and project rules to intentional, skill-driven engineering.

Key takeaways:

- Understand what IDE skills are and how they differ from prompts and static project rules.
- Learn how to design and structure effective skill files for Flutter projects.
- Apply skills to create a consistent, scalable, AI-assisted development workflow.

sessions.title: Become a Full + Video Stack Dev - Writing UIs is just the start
sessions.description:
What if you could use your existing Flutter skills to not only build UIs on screens, but to create visual stories ... and export them as real video files like MP4? 

From Spotify-Wrapped like summaries, over instructional content that evolves with your codebase, to personalized videos for tens of thousands of users ... all generated programmatically.

No video editor app, no ui clicking ... just beautiful code and open source! <3

# Batteries included: 

* collaboration & versioning in Git
* review changes via pull requests
* batch-generating variations
* customize videos on the fly
* or integrating video creation directly into pipelines and CI systems

In this talk, we dive into the interested and technical challenge of making this possible. We’ll explore how Flutter can create scenes, how layouts and animations are evaluated across time, and how pixels are captured and how time is relative when turned into deterministic video output. 

Along the way, we’ll discuss architectural decisions, performance considerations, and why Flutter and its skia/impeller engines turn out to be a surprisingly powerful foundation for video.

And yes, although optional, we will also discuss what unbelievable potential vibe coding videos has. 

Just think about telling Claude Code "take all images and information form this folder and turn it into a video". 
Your partner/dog/cat will always be in your debt for taking care of the "holiday video"! ;)
And you want to make your better half happy ... don't you?

Having worked with multiple code-to-video approaches and as the author of Flutter’s open-source fluvie, I’ll share both the opportunities and the engineering behind turning UI components/widgets into real video files.

This isn’t a tutorial or an AI hype talk. It’s a technical deep dive into how frontend frameworks work under the hood and how coding videos might actually even be better than using traditional tools.

sessions.title: Desktop windowing APIs
sessions.description: Multi-window support is one of the most highly anticipated features for Flutter desktop. In this talk, we will demo Flutter's new windowing features, dive into technical hurdles that needed to be overcome, and look under the hood to understand it all works.

sessions.title: Next steps and new patterns for GenUI
sessions.description: By now, you've likely heard about Generative UI, a UX architecture in which an agent not only generates content, but also reasons about how that content should be displayed and made interactive. In this talk, we'll go a step further than introductions and talk about more advanced topics like caching A2UI messages, combining local and agentic state, and more!

sessions.title: Beyond the Code: Identity, Burnout & Resilience
sessions.description:
Developers work in fast‑moving, high‑pressure environments where expectations change quickly and the cognitive load is constant. Even without writing code myself, I’ve spent years studying how people navigate change, burnout, and identity shifts in demanding roles. This session brings a human‑centered perspective to the Flutter community, offering space to reflect on the emotional side of building — the part that often gets overlooked in technical conversations.
We’ll explore what burnout actually feels like, why identity gets shaken during rapid growth or transition, and how individuals and teams can build resilience in sustainable, practical ways. This talk is designed to support the humans behind the code, giving attendees tools they can use immediately to feel more grounded, connected, and clear in their work.
Key Takeaways
- A simple, accessible framework for understanding burnout and how to interrupt it
- Why identity shifts happen during career changes, promotions, or high‑pressure cycles
- Practical resilience tools that don’t require extra time or energy to implement
- How to communicate more clearly during stress or uncertainty
- Ways to support teammates (and yourself) during periods of rapid change

sessions.title: Bridging Ecosystems: Embedding Flutter Components in a Web App
sessions.description:
Want to use your Flutter components inside an existing web app — one built and maintained by another team, in another stack? Flutter's Multi-View Web API makes it possible to embed multiple independent Flutter components into a host web page, where they run alongside the native web content as if they belonged there.
This talk is a practical guide to that API. We took an existing mobile Flutter package, made it web-compatible, and embedded its components into a host web app — and we'll walk you through exactly how it works: running multiple Flutter views on a single page, sharing one codebase across mobile and web, and using Dart's JS Interop for type-safe, two-way communication with the host site. We'll also share the trade-offs and lessons learned from doing it for real.
Key Takeaways:

Embed multiple distinct Flutter components into an existing web application at the same time.
Proven patterns for managing state and lifecycle across a multi-component Flutter web embed.
When to use multi-view mode vs. full-page mode — and why it matters.
Data-fetching and code-sharing strategies that keep mobile and web consistent.
How to handle cross-boundary errors and keep state in sync between the host web app and Flutter.

sessions.title: Meta-Flutter: Architecting a Package to Automate the IDE
sessions.description:
We know Flutter can build apps, but can we use Dart to automate the very tools we use to write it? This session takes you behind the scenes of a greenfield project: building a Flutter package that automates the creation of VS Code extensions. The goal? To allow developers to stay entirely within Dart while the package handles the heavy lifting of JS generation and API bridging.

We’ll move past simple interop to discuss the "Meta" challenges of package architecture. You’ll see the R&D process of orchestrating dart2js to output valid extension entry points, the struggle of defining a clean API for other developers, and the architectural "gymnastics" required to automate a cross-platform workflow.

sessions.title: Building a Production Viral Video Feed in Flutter
sessions.description:
Video playback in Flutter looks simple until you ship it. The framework gives you the building blocks, but a production-quality video feed demands answers to questions you didn't know to ask.

When we built Divine, a short-form video app, we hit every wall the ecosystem has to offer. This talk covers the architecture that got us past them.

Key takeaways:
- How Flutter's video player ecosystem actually stacks up, and why the choice of player library shapes everything that follows
- An architecture pattern for managing native video resources in a scrollable feed without hitting device limits
- Preloading and caching techniques that make swipe transitions feel instant
- Real-world platform divergence between iOS and Android that you won't find in documentation

sessions.title: Effective Dart Admin SDK in Firebase Dart Functions
sessions.description:
In this talk we are going to go over setting up Dart functions for Firebase and using the Firebase Dart Admin SDK to perform operations on your Firebase resources. Audience members will learn:

* How to quickly get started with Firebase Functions for Dart
* How to deploy functions
* How to use the admin sdk to operate on Firebase resources.

sessions.title: Bringing hot reload to your full Dart stack: backend, website, and database
sessions.description:
Hot reload is a defining feature of Flutter, but what if it didn’t stop at the user interface and extended all the way through your backend, database, website, and infrastructure? How much more enjoyable would your developer experience be? How much faster could you get your app to market?

In this session, we’ll explore how we brought true hot reload to the Dart server, enabling seamless iteration across the entire stack. From updating business logic on the fly and generating code to applying database migrations while the server is running, we’ll demonstrate a completely new development workflow where nothing needs to stop, and everything always stays in sync.

We’ll dive into the technical details of how hot reload works under the hood, including code reloading strategies, state preservation, and safely evolving schemas without downtime. You’ll see how these pieces connect to create a cohesive, full-stack developer experience powered entirely by Dart.

We’ll also explore how this unlocks a new level of productivity when combined with agentic coding workflows. By integrating with an MCP server and carefully crafted skills, agents can generate, apply, and evolve database migrations and backend logic in real time. All while your app keeps running.

Whether you’re building rapid prototypes or large-scale production systems, this approach fundamentally changes how you iterate on full-stack apps.

sessions.title: Flight Mode AI: Building Local LLM Apps Easily with LlamaDart
sessions.description:
We are used to "high confidence" coding with cloud models like Gemini, but what happens when the WiFi cuts out at 30,000 feet? I started building a writing assistant for myself and quickly realized that true reliability meant going local. But integrating llama.cpp—the gold standard for local inference—usually means entering "dependency hell," managing complex C++ builds across Linux, Windows, Android, and iOS.

In this talk, I will show you how llamadart(https://pub.dev/packages/llamadart) solves this. By leveraging modern Dart 3.10 build hooks and GitHub Actions, I’ve automated the heavy lifting: the library detects your platform and auto-downloads the correct pre-compiled binary at build time.

We will move beyond the low-level architecture and focus on the developer experience available right now. I’ll demonstrate how easy it is to spin up an offline AI:

Zero Configuration: No CMake, no NDKs, no manual compilation.

High-Level APIs: Initialize a model and start chatting with just a few lines of Dart code.

Auto-Templating: Forget manual string formatting; let the library handle the chat templates for you.

Whether you want to build a privacy-first assistant or just ensure your app works in "Flight Mode," you’ll leave this session ready to build your first offline AI app today.

sessions.title: Designer + Engineer + Claude: Building a Cross-Functional AI Workflow from Figma to Flutter
sessions.description:
Elevator pitch: The handoff between design and engineering is where most Flutter products lose fidelity, time, and trust. We'll show how a VGV product designer and engineer used Claude end-to-end on a real client engagement by merging two native design systems into one AI-readable Figma system, then generating theme-integrated, golden-tested Flutter widgets that match design intent pixel-for-pixel.

Description (~250 words):
Most "AI in Flutter" talks are told from one side of the wall: an engineer using a model to write code faster. The interesting workflow lives on both sides of that wall, and across it.In this session, a VGV lead product designer and engineering lead walk through a real client engagement where Claude became a shared collaborator across the full design-to-code pipeline.
  • Refining the design system for AI readability: Before a line of code was written, we used Claude to interrogate, restructure, and improve an existing Figma system. Think token naming, component conventions, variant structure, documentation. Not just for human clarity, but for machine readability. This included establishing AI readability best practices that made downstream code generation dramatically more reliable.
  • Claude Code inside Figma: Using Claude Code via the Figma Desktop Bridge MCP, allowed us to accelerate the design system refinement itself. By enabling this, Claude had the ability to directly edit inside Figma in real time: bulk renaming, correcting inconsistencies, writing documentation, creating variables. Fast, precise, and collaborative.
  • Generating the specs: Turning Figma artifacts into structured, machine-readable specifications engineering can actually consume.
  • Implementing in Flutter: A Figma-to-Flutter agent skill that reads design tokens directly via the Figma MCP server, generates theme-integrated widgets, and validates pixel fidelity with LLM driven visual comparison, and Widgetbook use cases for human validation.
  • VGV good practices: Feature-First Clean Architecture, Widgetbook, Very Good CLI scaffolding, and the guardrails that let AI accelerate without eroding quality.You'll leave with a concrete blueprint for a cross-functional AI workflow (not a demo reel), honest lessons from what works and what doesn't yet, and a clear sense of why pairing a product designer and engineer on AI tooling produces something neither gets to alone. Built on a live client engagement, presented by the people who actually shipped it.

What attendees will learn
  • A repeatable pattern for using AI in the design system definition phase, before engineering starts.
  • How to structure Figma so an agent can consume it reliably, including token, component, and variant conventions.
  • The mechanics of a Figma-to-Flutter skill: MCP integration, code generation, theme integration, golden-test validation.
  • Where the human stays in the loop on both the design and engineering sides, and why that matters.
  • How this fits into Feature-First Clean Architecture and the wider VGV agentic engineering practice.

sessions.title: Full Stack AI features
sessions.description: Learn how to quickly integrate powerful AI capabilities into your Flutter applications. We’ll explore the seamless connection between Firebase’s scalable AI infrastructure and Genkit’s flexible AI framework to build production-ready features.

sessions.title: Hook, Line, and Sinker: Dart Hooks and FFI in Production
sessions.description:
Dart Hooks and FFIGen promise cleaner, more automatic native library integration and they deliver, once you get past what the docs don't cover. I'll share what I learned integrating a pre-compiled C++ SDK into Flutter for Android and iOS. The docs assume best-case scenarios and real projects rarely look like that. We'll cover the gaps, the gotchas, and the mental models that make it all click.

In this talk you will learn:

1) The docs assume you have source files. You probably don't. FFIGen examples focus on bundling .c and .h files directly. When you're working with pre-compiled .so and .dylib binaries, you have to piece together a different approach. We'll cover exactly how to structure that.

2) CodeAsset.name is more important than it looks. The connection between your hook/build.dart asset name and the @Native annotations generated by FFIGen is a critical and poorly documented contract. Get it wrong and you get silent runtime failures. We'll demystify exactly how these map to each other.

3) Bundle the whole dependency chain, not just the main library. If your .so depends on other .so files, Android's dynamic linker needs them all present at runtime. We'll look at how to use nm and readelf to discover those dependencies and bundle them correctly.

sessions.title: The JavaScript Exit: Flutter Web Beyond JavaScript
sessions.description:
Everyone knows Flutter Web as “Canvas, bundles, and compromises.” This talk asks a more interesting question: what changes when Flutter Web stops being only a JavaScript story and starts leaning on WebAssembly? Flutter supports building web apps with --wasm, falls back to JavaScript where needed, and can render faster with less jank when multithreading is available.

I’ll show what this means in practice: which apps benefit, where browser compatibility still gets messy, how JS interop changes for Wasm-compatible apps, and why package:web and dart:js_interop matter more than many Flutter teams realize. This is not a generic web talk; it is a practical guide to deciding whether Flutter Web is entering a new phase or just getting a new buzzword.

sessions.title: Bare Metal Flutter: Raspberry Pi, GPIO, and the Embedded Frontier
sessions.description:
Your Flutter app is about to get physical!

This technical deep dive moves Flutter off the phone and onto single-board hardware… Specifically the Raspberry Pi. You'll learn how flutter-pi works, how to get it running, how to read and write GPIO pins from Dart, and how to optimize your system for embedded deployment.

Bonus content for the hardware-curious: a first look at designing a custom Pi HAT — taking your project from "wires everywhere" to "actually shippable"

Fair warning: after this talk, you're going to need a Raspberry Pi.

sessions.title: Building Modern Web Apps with Jaspr, Gemini and Antigravity
sessions.description:
Flutter Web brought Dart to the browser by reimagining the web as a canvas. Jaspr takes a different path.

In Building Modern Web Apps with Jaspr, we'll introduce a framework that works with the web platform rather than around it. We will use Gemini, Antigravity, and Jaspr Skills to speed up our development. You'll learn how Jaspr leverages HTML, CSS, and server-side rendering while keeping the developer experience familiar to Dart and Flutter developers.

This Intermediate session is also beginner-friendly and will walk through core concepts, show how to build a simple app, and highlight the key differences from Flutter Web. Whether you're exploring alternatives or just getting started with web development in Dart, this talk will give you a practical, approachable entry point to Jaspr.

sessions.title: Building an Integrated AI-Powered Development Stack
sessions.description:
Most teams adopt AI coding assistants and discover the same thing within a month. The output is fast, the output is plausible, the output is wrong in ways that take longer to fix than writing it yourself. The gap isn't the model. It's everything around the model. This talk is about what fills that gap.
We'll walk through the architecture of a production AI engineering system, drawn from our experience building, shipping, and rebuilding our own at Very Good Ventures. The frame is layered. At the bottom, contextual knowledge that teaches the AI your standards. In the middle, structured workflows that replace ad-hoc prompting with brainstorm, plan, and build phases. At the top, guardrails through hooks, MCP integrations, and automated analysis that catch problems before they reach a PR. Each layer answers a different failure mode, and skipping any of them is where most teams stall.
The talk covers the design decisions that actually mattered. Why we moved from monolithic prompts to discrete skills. Why brainstorming and planning needed to be separate phases instead of one step. What we got wrong about tool-agnosticism the first time, and what changed when we tried again. How to know when a convention belongs in AI context versus a linter versus a code review checklist. The patterns generalize beyond Flutter, but we'll ground every principle in real examples from Flutter work so the lessons are concrete, not abstract.
You'll leave with a model for how the pieces fit together, a clearer sense of which layer to invest in first for your team, and a few opinionated takes on what to avoid.

Key Learnings
- A layered model for AI-assisted development, contextual knowledge, structured workflows, and automated guardrails, and why each layer matters
- How to decompose a feature into brainstorm, plan, and build phases that produce better output than direct prompting
- How to encode team standards as contextual skills the AI actually reaches for, versus standards that get ignored
- Where hooks and MCP integrations belong in the loop, and where they add friction without value
- Practical guidance for sequencing adoption so quality and velocity move together instead of trading off

sessions.title: Self-Healing Flutter UI with Widgetbook: Teaching Coding Agents to Judge Their Own Work
sessions.description:
Coding agents generate Flutter UI fast. The hard problem isn't speed anymore, it's quality. Agents ship pull requests where the code compiles, the tests pass, and the widget looks right at first glance. But the design system has been quietly violated. The layout breaks on a small phone nobody tested. The accessibility label exists but doesn't actually help a screen reader user. Reviewers don't have time to catch all of it. UI debt accumulates at twice the speed.
This talk shows a workflow that flips the problem. The agent verifies its own work before a human ever sees the PR. We use Flutter's structured trees (the Widget Tree and the Semantics Tree) as ground truth. Widgetbook turns those trees into compact, actionable diffs that an agent can read, reason about, and fix in a tight loop. Then, and only then, does the human get a clean, summarized PR to review. Code changes can be reviewed in Git while UI changes can be reviewed in Widgetbook Cloud.

Widgetbook's customer Couchsurfing will join the stage to showcase how they have been using Widgetbook.

Walk away with a complete UI agent-assisted development for Flutter: how to give your design system to an agent as context, how to build self-healing test loops with flutter test, and how to review your agents work.

sessions.title: Next-level Generative AI ::: Moving Beyond Copilots to a Multi-Agent Autonomous Workforce
sessions.description:
Subtitle: ; How they work and why they are good for your career.

**The Strategic Pivot: AI Augmentation Inversion**

The first wave of AI adoption has focused on AI-Augmented Humans—equipping existing staff with "Copilots" to marginally increase speed. From the perspective of the C-Suite, this approach has largely yielded disappointing results, as human cognitive bottlenecks and legacy workflows remain the primary constraints. From the engineering perspective, the quality of the generated code was unreliable and often disappointing.

**The True Organizational Transformation**

Substantial improvements in productivity and cost-efficiency requires an inversion of AI augmentation to create Human-Augmented AI. We are moving beyond "tools" and into the era of the Autonomous Virtual Workforce. In this model, a small number of highly talented humans act as Agentic Workforce Designers and Managers, augmenting a domain-specific virtual workforce staffed with dozens of highly trained agents who work together to perform nearly all the cognitive labor. 

Rather than AI augmenting humans, the high-impact transformation occurs when the primary model is a small number of human professionalss augmenting an autonomous agentic workforce (*human-in-the-middle*).

KEY TAKEAWAYS
1) Key aspects of why an agentic workforce like the Flutter & GCP VCoE™ works so well
2) How to build your own virtual Flutter workforce
3) The redefinition of our role as accomplished Flutter professionals integrated with an agentic workforce, and *why that makes us more valuable as professionals*

sessions.title: Don’t Break the Brand: Maintaining a Design System in Flutter
sessions.description:
Building reusable widgets in Flutter is easy. Building a design system that scales and stays consistent over time is not.

As applications grow, design systems often turn into “widget dumping grounds”: inconsistent APIs, duplicated styles, and components that slowly drift away from the original design intent. Without clear boundaries, a solid theming foundation, and proper validation workflows, even well-intentioned systems become hard to use and harder to trust.

Drawing from our experience building and evolving the design system behind the Holafly app, we’ll share real-world challenges, trade-offs, and lessons learned while scaling a production UI system.

In this talk, we’ll move beyond simple widget construction to explore the architecture of a healthy, dedicated UI package. We will cover:
- Crafting widget APIs that are flexible, consistent, and easy to adopt across teams.
- Structuring tokens for colors, typography, and spacing that provide a cohesive foundation for your Design System.
- Implementing practical strategies to protect the brand, including widget and Golden tests for visual regression, and the use of component galleries or tools like Widgetbook to document, visualize and validate the UI in isolation.

Whether you’re starting a design system from scratch or trying to regain control of an existing one, joining us in this talk will give you practical tools to ensure your code remains a source of truth rather than a source of frustration.

sessions.title: Flutter on Foldables: How hard could it be?
sessions.description:
Foldable devices are no longer a gimmick—they are a form factor. But for cross-platform developers, they represent a terrifying variable: dynamic screen sizes, physical hinges, and exotic "postures" like Tabletop mode.

In this talk, we will bridge the gap between native Android mechanics and Flutter widgets. We’ll look under the hood to see how Flutter wraps Jetpack WindowManager, compare the Kotlin vs. Dart implementation of hinge detection, and explore the dual_screen ecosystem. Whether you are an Android dev skeptical of Flutter’s hardware access, or a Flutter dev terrified of the "crease," you’ll leave knowing exactly how hard—or easy—it is to unfold your app.

Key takeaways:
- Learn exactly how Flutter wraps the native Jetpack WindowManager internally, proving that cross-platform doesn't mean losing 'hardware access'.
- See a side-by-side code comparison (Kotlin vs. Dart) of how to detect if a device is flat, folded, or in "Tabletop mode" (90°).
- Understand the difference between the dual_screen ecosystem for hinge-aware mechanics and standard Flutter responsiveness.
- Discover "how hard it actually is" to support dynamic screen sizes and the physical crease, moving from skepticism to implementation.

sessions.title: From Lottie to Flutter: How Etsy Rebuilt Year in Review with Flutter Native Animations
sessions.description:
Etsy's Year in Review is one of the most animation-heavy moments in the app. For years it shipped on Lottie. This year, the team rebuilt it entirely with native Flutter animations, and dropped a third-party dependency in the process.

This talk is the story of that migration, told by the engineer who shipped it and the trainer who helped the team get there. Filip will walk through the technical decisions: which Lottie compositions translated cleanly to AnimationController and Tween, which ones needed CustomPainter or implicit animations, and where Flutter's animation primitives forced the team to think differently about motion design. Jorge will cover the enablement side: how a team that had leaned on Lottie for years built fluency in native Flutter animation fast enough to ship a flagship feature on it.

You'll leave with a concrete decision framework for when to drop Lottie, a tour of the Flutter animation patterns that did the heavy lifting in Year in Review, and a realistic picture of what it takes to upskill a production team on motion. Most useful for engineers and engineering leaders weighing the same tradeoff on their own apps.

sessions.title: Next-level Generative AI ::: Moving Beyond Copilots to a Multi-Agent Autonomous Workforce (Roundtabl
sessions.description:
Subtitle: ; How they work and why they are good for your career.

**The Strategic Pivot: AI Augmentation Inversion**

The first wave of AI adoption has focused on AI-Augmented Humans—equipping existing staff with "Copilots" to marginally increase speed. From the perspective of the C-Suite, this approach has largely yielded disappointing results, as human cognitive bottlenecks and legacy workflows remain the primary constraints. From the engineering perspective, the quality of the generated code was unreliable and often disappointing.

**The True Organizational Transformation**

Substantial improvements in productivity and cost-efficiency requires an inversion of AI augmentation to create Human-Augmented AI. We are moving beyond "tools" and into the era of the Autonomous Virtual Workforce. In this model, a small number of highly talented humans act as Agentic Workforce Designers and Managers, augmenting a domain-specific virtual workforce staffed with dozens of highly trained agents who work together to perform nearly all the cognitive labor. 

Rather than AI augmenting humans, the high-impact transformation occurs when the primary model is a small number of human professionalss augmenting an autonomous agentic workforce (*human-in-the-middle*).

KEY TAKEAWAYS
1) Key aspects of why an agentic workforce like the Flutter & GCP VCoE™ works so well
2) How to build your own virtual Flutter workforce
3) The redefinition of our role as accomplished Flutter professionals integrated with an agentic workforce, and *why that makes us more valuable as professionals*

sessions.title: Your Next Flutter Teammate Is Not Human
sessions.description:
AI is rapidly becoming part of the Flutter workflow, but not in the way it is usually presented in conference talks. This is not about adding a chatbot to your app; it is about a deeper shift: AI is starting to behave like a teammate inside the development loop.

In this roundtable, we will explore what happens when prompt-based tools step into tasks like UI scaffolding, repetitive coding, test generation, refactoring, documentation, and debugging. We will compare real experiences: where these tools genuinely save time, where they introduce risks or false confidence, and how blindly accepting generated code can actually degrade a codebase instead of improving it.

The goal is not to promote AI hype or reject it, but to build a more grounded, shared perspective: how can Flutter teams work with these tools while maintaining speed, critical thinking, and strong engineering judgment?

sessions.title: Hot Ones: The Spicy Truth About Migrating to Flutter
sessions.description:
Hot Ones: The Spicy Truth About Migrating to Flutter.

Migrating to Flutter in an enterprise context.

sessions.title: Is Flutter a Smart Bet in an AI-Driven Job Market?
sessions.description:
Artificial intelligence is rapidly changing the job market, and many students and professionals are asking difficult questions: What skills will still matter tomorrow? Which technologies are worth investing time in? How do we avoid becoming obsolete as AI continues to reshape the industry?

This roundtable opens a discussion around the role of Flutter in this changing landscape. As entry-level roles evolve and traditional career paths become less predictable, choosing the right tools and technologies is no longer just a technical decision, it’s a strategic one.

Together, we’ll explore whether Flutter’s cross-platform approach, versatility, and broad application across mobile, web, desktop, and emerging experiences can make it a valuable foundation for an uncertain future. Is it better to specialize deeply in one niche, or build adaptable skills around tools that allow you to move across different domains? What role does Flutter play in a world where AI is changing how software is built? And in a future shaped by agentic coding, does the framework itself even matter anymore, or will understanding products, systems, and problem-solving become more important than the tools we use?

Expect an informal conversation with different perspectives, experiences, and practical insights rather than a one-way presentation.

sessions.title: Beyond Code: Becoming a High-Impact Flutter Engineer
sessions.description:
What makes a great Flutter engineer? 

Most people immediately think of writing clean code, mastering state management, or building scalable architectures. While technical excellence is the foundation, it isn’t the ceiling. In real-world teams, impact goes far beyond the codebase.

In this interactive roundtable, we’ll explore what it truly means to grow from a developer into a high-impact contributor. We’ll share lessons learned from rebuilding and scaling our apps, highlighting how engineers can amplify their impact on the code, the product, and the team.

Because great engineers don’t just build features, they elevate everything around them.

sessions.title: What Should We Measure in the Age of Agentic Engineering?
sessions.description:
As AI-assisted and agentic development become standard, traditional engineering metrics are losing their meaning. Story points, PR counts, and velocity warp when teams use AI to generate code, tests, and documentation. While agentic tools unlock unprecedented capacity, they introduce a new risk: accelerating the delivery of low-value or low-quality work. If building software is no longer the bottleneck, how do we measure true engineering success?

This roundtable explores how mobile engineering leaders must redefine metrics in an era of agentic development. We will debate whether traditional KPIs now reward the wrong behaviors and discuss shifting our focus from raw output to quality, developer experience, and customer impact.

Topics for Discussion:

The Metrics Mirage: Are story points, PR counts, and velocity obsolete, or just changing?

Measuring AI Impact: How do we quantify the real ROI of AI-assisted development without rewarding output over user value?

Quality & Guardrails: How do we prevent AI from accelerating poorly designed features or inflating our support burden?

The New Signals: Should we be indexing purely on release confidence, lead time, and developer experience?

sessions.title: How to Lead High-Stakes Initiatives Through Ambiguity
sessions.description:
Leadership announces a high-visibility initiative. The vision is bold but vague. Nothing is defined yet, and everyone is watching. Your job as a lead is not just to strategize the goal, it is to strategize the delivery: what to build first, what to compromise, and how to keep momentum when the pressure stays high. This talk shares practical lessons from leading multiple high-stakes strategic launches, focusing on the parts most talks skip: turning ambiguity into a credible plan, shipping incrementally when leadership is watching, and communicating your work to people who do not care about the architecture.

Below are some of the questions we'll cover

> How do you translate a vague executive vision into a delivery plan that goes beyond a compelling demo?

> What does it take to align teams and secure buy-in across org boundaries when nothing is fully defined?

> How do you make smart trade-offs on competing priorities without losing momentum?

> How do you communicate technical complexity to directors and C-suite leaders without losing substance?

> What strategies help you navigate resource constraints, corporate layers, and the human bottlenecks that slow everything down?

sessions.title: Still Technical: The Return of the Engineering Leader
sessions.description:
For many engineers in the late 2010s and early 2020s, becoming an engineering manager felt like the natural next step. You grew as an engineer, started leading projects, mentored people, and eventually moved into management. But after 2025, the career ladder started to look different.

Headcount growth slowed down. Many companies flattened their organizations. At the same time, AI tools made hands-on builders dramatically more productive. A senior technical person who understands product, people, execution, and the tech stack can now operate as a true force multiplier.

In this talk, I’ll share what it looks and feels like to move between engineering management and individual contributor work — and how to turn that transition into a career advantage.

We’ll cover:
1. How small and large companies support IC ↔ EM transitions in 2026
2. How to ramp up fast and add value after being a hands-off manager for 1, 2, 5, or even 10 years
3. Which engineering management skills transfer to managing AI coding agents
4. How to scale success and set career goals after returning to hands-on building

sessions.title: Congratulations, You're Staff. Now Why Are You Still Stuck?
sessions.description:
You survived the grind to Staff or Lead. You are technically excellent. You are holding the entire codebase together with your bare hands and honestly a little bit of spite. And yet somehow — somehow — the promotion to Sr. Staff or Principal isn't happening, leadership keeps looking past you, and you can't figure out why. I can. And I'm going to tell you, with love and zero sugarcoating, that the things keeping you stuck are the exact skills that got you here. Let's fix it.

Picture this: You are a Staff Engineer. Or maybe they call you a Lead. Or a Senior Lead. Or a Senior Lead II, because your company ran out of words. You have been at this level for — let's not say how long. You are the person your team calls when something is on fire. You are the person who knows why that one service does that one thing that nobody else remembers. You have opinions about architecture that are correct, and you've been told they're correct, and then watched someone with a fancier title make the wrong call anyway while you quietly fixed it at 11pm.

You are excellent. And you are stuck.

Here's the thing nobody is going to put in your performance review: the habits and coping mechanisms that made you indispensable at Senior are actively hiding your readiness for the next level from the people who decide if you get there. The quiet heroics. The "I'll just fix it myself." The aversion to the visibility that feels like self-promotion but is actually just your job now. The scoping yourself to the team in front of you when the role requires you to be thinking three teams ahead. The way you've learned to survive a broken system — and how that survival mode looks, from the outside, exactly like someone who isn't ready to lead at scale.

We're going to talk about all of it. Specifically, we're going to name the behaviors:
The Hero Anti-Pattern: Or, Why Being Indispensable Is a Career Trap
If your team cannot function without you, leadership doesn't see a Principal candidate — they see a risk they cannot afford to promote away. We'll talk about why the instinct to just absorb the problem is the thing that's keeping you exactly where you are, and what to do instead.
Invisible Wins: The World's Least Efficient Strategy
You shipped something incredible. You know it. Your team knows it. Does your skip-level know it? Does the VP? Because if the answer is no, it didn't happen for your career. Making your work visible is not bragging — at the Staff+ level, it is the job. We'll talk about how to do it without becoming that person.
The Scope Blindspot
Staff engineers who are ready to be Sr. Staff or Principal are already thinking like Principals. They're not waiting for the title to start operating at the next scope. If you are solving the problems directly in front of you and waiting to be asked about the bigger ones, leadership reads that as ceiling, not humility. We'll dig into what it actually looks like to expand your scope intentionally and visibly before you have permission.
"I Don't Do Politics": The Most Expensive Thing You've Ever Said
Influence without authority is the core skill above Staff. It requires relationship capital, communication across functions you don't control, and yes — some amount of navigating organizational dynamics that feel gross and shouldn't be necessary but absolutely are. We'll talk about how to build real influence without selling your soul, because those are not the same thing.
The Skills That Actually Unlock Principal and Beyond
Technical depth got you here. Technical breadth, cross-functional fluency, and the ability to shape organizational direction — not just codebase direction — get you to Sr. Staff, Principal, Distinguished, and maybe even CTO one day. We'll get specific about what those skills are, how to develop them, and how to start demonstrating them now, in your current role, without waiting for someone to give you a project that justifies it.
This is a talk about getting unstuck. It's also a little bit of a confession, because some of these traps? I built them myself. The good news is I know how to get out of them. And I will not let you spend another two years staring at a promotion that should have happened already.

session.title: Registration & Check-In
Flutter is everywhere
session.description:
As Flutter continues to expand its reach—powering everything from automotive multimedia systems to the next generation of agentic applications—the framework is evolving to meet the needs of a global, distributed community and ecosystem.

Beyond the code, we will explore the critical role of community-driven governance in sustaining Flutter's growth. With Canonical now serving as a lead maintainer for multi-window desktop support, we will discuss how Flutter is shifting toward a more decentralized governance model to ensure the framework remains "built by everyone, for everyone". Join us to see how we are reimagining the developer experience for an AI-driven world through Agentic Hot Reload and GenUI, and learn how you can contribute to the roadmap that will shape the future of cross-platform development.

session.title: Break
Flutter at Scale: A Roadmap for the Modern Enterprise
session.description: With countless companies across every industry running Flutter in production, the conversation has shifted from “What can Flutter do?” to “How do we maximize its impact at scale?” In this opening keynote, Flutter Outbound PM Abdallah Shaban and VGV CEO David DeRemer explore Flutter’s next chapter. They will dive into how Flutter and the broader ecosystem of contributors are investing to meet enterprise demands, and the emerging market patterns VGV sees as teams scale their Flutter footprint.

session.title: Making AI Predictable: Skill-Driven Flutter Development
session.description:
AI coding has evolved beyond one-off prompts. Modern IDEs now support structured, reusable skills — persistent behaviors that shape how your tools think, generate, and refactor code.

In this talk, we’ll explore what skills actually are, how they differ from traditional prompting, and how to integrate them into your Flutter development workflow. You’ll learn how to design skill files, structure them effectively, and use them to standardize architecture, enforce patterns, and accelerate real-world Flutter projects.

By the end, you’ll understand how to move from prompts and project rules to intentional, skill-driven engineering.

Key takeaways:

- Understand what IDE skills are and how they differ from prompts and static project rules.
- Learn how to design and structure effective skill files for Flutter projects.
- Apply skills to create a consistent, scalable, AI-assisted development workflow.

session.title: Effective Dart Admin SDK in Firebase Dart Functions
session.description:
In this talk we are going to go over setting up Dart functions for Firebase and using the Firebase Dart Admin SDK to perform operations on your Firebase resources. Audience members will learn:

* How to quickly get started with Firebase Functions for Dart
* How to deploy functions
* How to use the admin sdk to operate on Firebase resources.

session.title: Bare Metal Flutter: Raspberry Pi, GPIO, and the Embedded Frontier
session.description:
Your Flutter app is about to get physical!

This technical deep dive moves Flutter off the phone and onto single-board hardware… Specifically the Raspberry Pi. You'll learn how flutter-pi works, how to get it running, how to read and write GPIO pins from Dart, and how to optimize your system for embedded deployment.

Bonus content for the hardware-curious: a first look at designing a custom Pi HAT — taking your project from "wires everywhere" to "actually shippable"

Fair warning: after this talk, you're going to need a Raspberry Pi.

session.description:
As AI-assisted and agentic development become standard, traditional engineering metrics are losing their meaning. Story points, PR counts, and velocity warp when teams use AI to generate code, tests, and documentation. While agentic tools unlock unprecedented capacity, they introduce a new risk: accelerating the delivery of low-value or low-quality work. This roundtable explores how mobile engineering leaders must redefine metrics in an era of agentic development. We will debate whether traditional KPIs now reward the wrong behaviors and discuss shifting our focus from raw output to quality, developer experience, and customer impact.

Topics for Discussion:

The Metrics Mirage: Are story points, PR counts, and velocity obsolete, or just changing?

Measuring AI Impact: How do we quantify the real ROI of AI-assisted development without rewarding output over user value?

Quality & Guardrails: How do we prevent AI from accelerating poorly designed features or inflating our support burden?

The New Signals: Should we be indexing purely on release confidence, lead time, and developer experience?

session.title: Break
Flutter Migration at Scale: Migrate Tamara App in 10 Weeks Journey
session.description:
What does it really take to migrate a large-scale, Fintech, and used by millions production app to Flutter—fast, safely, and without breaking the business or user trust?

In this talk, I'm gonna share the behind-the-scenes story of how the Tamara app was migrated from native to Flutter in just 10 weeks. This isn’t a “hello world” migration—this is real-world engineering under pressure: undocumented business logic buried in Jira tickets, teams with mixed backgrounds, and the constant challenge of moving fast without things catching fire.

From convincing stakeholders to trust the move, to onboarding engineers and building internal tooling powered by AI, this session dives deep into the practical decisions, trade-offs, and battle-tested strategies that made the migration possible.

If you’re thinking about Flutter at scale—or already in the middle of the chaos—this talk will give you a realistic playbook (with a few scars and lessons along the way).

session.title: Become a Full + Video Stack Dev - Writing UIs is just the start
session.description:
What if you could use your existing Flutter skills to not only build UIs on screens, but to create visual stories ... and export them as real video files like MP4? 

From Spotify-Wrapped like summaries, over instructional content that evolves with your codebase, to personalized videos for tens of thousands of users ... all generated programmatically.

No video editor app, no ui clicking ... just beautiful code and open source! <3

# Batteries included: 

* collaboration & versioning in Git
* review changes via pull requests
* batch-generating variations
* customize videos on the fly
* or integrating video creation directly into pipelines and CI systems

In this talk, we dive into the interested and technical challenge of making this possible. We’ll explore how Flutter can create scenes, how layouts and animations are evaluated across time, and how pixels are captured and how time is relative when turned into deterministic video output. 

Along the way, we’ll discuss architectural decisions, performance considerations, and why Flutter and its skia/impeller engines turn out to be a surprisingly powerful foundation for video.

And yes, although optional, we will also discuss what unbelievable potential vibe coding videos has. 

Just think about telling Claude Code "take all images and information form this folder and turn it into a video". 
Your partner/dog/cat will always be in your debt for taking care of the "holiday video"! ;)
And you want to make your better half happy ... don't you?

Having worked with multiple code-to-video approaches and as the author of Flutter’s open-source fluvie, I’ll share both the opportunities and the engineering behind turning UI components/widgets into real video files.

This isn’t a tutorial or an AI hype talk. It’s a technical deep dive into how frontend frameworks work under the hood and how coding videos might actually even be better than using traditional tools.

session.title: Bringing hot reload to your full Dart stack: backend, website, and database
session.description:
Hot reload is a defining feature of Flutter, but what if it didn’t stop at the user interface and extended all the way through your backend, database, website, and infrastructure? How much more enjoyable would your developer experience be? How much faster could you get your app to market?

In this session, we’ll explore how we brought true hot reload to the Dart server, enabling seamless iteration across the entire stack. From updating business logic on the fly and generating code to applying database migrations while the server is running, we’ll demonstrate a completely new development workflow where nothing needs to stop, and everything always stays in sync.

We’ll dive into the technical details of how hot reload works under the hood, including code reloading strategies, state preservation, and safely evolving schemas without downtime. You’ll see how these pieces connect to create a cohesive, full-stack developer experience powered entirely by Dart.

We’ll also explore how this unlocks a new level of productivity when combined with agentic coding workflows. By integrating with an MCP server and carefully crafted skills, agents can generate, apply, and evolve database migrations and backend logic in real time. All while your app keeps running.

Whether you’re building rapid prototypes or large-scale production systems, this approach fundamentally changes how you iterate on full-stack apps.

session.title: Building Modern Web Apps with Jaspr, Gemini and Antigravity
session.description:
Flutter Web brought Dart to the browser by reimagining the web as a canvas. Jaspr takes a different path.

In Building Modern Web Apps with Jaspr, we'll introduce a framework that works with the web platform rather than around it. We will use Gemini, Antigravity, and Jaspr Skills to speed up our development. You'll learn how Jaspr leverages HTML, CSS, and server-side rendering while keeping the developer experience familiar to Dart and Flutter developers.

This Intermediate session is also beginner-friendly and will walk through core concepts, show how to build a simple app, and highlight the key differences from Flutter Web. Whether you're exploring alternatives or just getting started with web development in Dart, this talk will give you a practical, approachable entry point to Jaspr.

session.description:
Leadership announces a high-visibility initiative. The vision is bold but vague. Nothing is defined yet, and everyone is watching. Your job as a lead is not just to strategize the goal, it is to strategize the delivery: what to build first, what to compromise, and how to keep momentum when the pressure stays high. This talk shares practical lessons from leading multiple high-stakes strategic launches, focusing on the parts most talks skip: turning ambiguity into a credible plan, shipping incrementally when leadership is watching, and communicating your work to people who do not care about the architecture.

Below are some of the questions we'll cover

> How do you translate a vague executive vision into a delivery plan that goes beyond a compelling demo?

> What does it take to align teams and secure buy-in across org boundaries when nothing is fully defined?

> How do you make smart trade-offs on competing priorities without losing momentum?

> How do you communicate technical complexity to directors and C-suite leaders without losing substance?

> What strategies help you navigate resource constraints, corporate layers, and the human bottlenecks that slow everything down?

session.title: Break
From Vibe-Coded Viral MVP to Launchable Product in 90 Days
session.description:
What happens when a viral app built with AI-generated code needs to become a real, shippable product fast?

This is the story of Divine, a groundbreaking short-form video platform and a reboot of the original Vine — the iconic app acquired by Twitter — now reimagined for a decentralized world and backed by Jack Dorsey. Its CEO had built an exciting MVP using vibe coding, but turning that into a stable, production-grade Flutter app was a different challenge entirely. That's where Very Good Ventures came in.

In this session, the Divine and the VGV team will share the inside story of how we went from a codebase with 500+ open issues and no CI/CD infrastructure to a live product on Zapstore — in 90 days. We'll walk through the strategic decisions, the engineering methodology, and the human side of running a high-velocity product engagement in the age of AI.

Key Takeaways:
- From vibe-coded to production cadence: assessing and stabilizing AI-generated codebases: We'll share the assessment framework VGV used to triage 400+ test cases and classify what was working, partially working, broken, and not yet built, giving founders and engineering leads a replicable model for inherited or AI-generated codebases. We'll also walk through how a "Stability-First" roadmap helped align a fast-moving founder with an engineering team focused on long-term quality, and how daily transparency built the trust needed to make hard trade-offs fast.

- Agentic Flutter Engineering in practice: Learn how VGV embedded AI agents (including Claude Code) as active co-developers and code reviewers throughout the entire SDLC as a structured methodology that compressed delivery timelines without sacrificing architectural integrity.

- Building on NOSTR: Flutter meets decentralized social: Divine is built on NOSTR, an open, decentralized protocol for social media that is rapidly gaining traction as an alternative to centralized platforms. Its event-driven, cryptographic architecture requires a different mental model than traditional REST or GraphQL APIs. We'll share how VGV leveraged existing NOSTR knowledge within the team to integrate it cleanly within Flutter, and why decentralized protocols are becoming an increasingly valuable skill for mobile engineers building the next generation of social apps.

- What vibe coding means for the Flutter ecosystem: A candid conversation between a founder who built with it and an engineering firm that inherited it: the opportunities, the risks, and what comes next as AI-generated Flutter apps become more common.

session.title: Desktop windowing APIs
session.description: Multi-window support is one of the most highly anticipated features for Flutter desktop. In this talk, we will demo Flutter's new windowing features, dive into technical hurdles that needed to be overcome, and look under the hood to understand it all works.

session.title: Flight Mode AI: Building Local LLM Apps Easily with LlamaDart
session.description:
We are used to "high confidence" coding with cloud models like Gemini, but what happens when the WiFi cuts out at 30,000 feet? I started building a writing assistant for myself and quickly realized that true reliability meant going local. But integrating llama.cpp—the gold standard for local inference—usually means entering "dependency hell," managing complex C++ builds across Linux, Windows, Android, and iOS.

In this talk, I will show you how llamadart(https://pub.dev/packages/llamadart) solves this. By leveraging modern Dart 3.10 build hooks and GitHub Actions, I’ve automated the heavy lifting: the library detects your platform and auto-downloads the correct pre-compiled binary at build time.

We will move beyond the low-level architecture and focus on the developer experience available right now. I’ll demonstrate how easy it is to spin up an offline AI:

Zero Configuration: No CMake, no NDKs, no manual compilation.

High-Level APIs: Initialize a model and start chatting with just a few lines of Dart code.

Auto-Templating: Forget manual string formatting; let the library handle the chat templates for you.

Whether you want to build a privacy-first assistant or just ensure your app works in "Flight Mode," you’ll leave this session ready to build your first offline AI app today.

session.title: Building an Integrated AI-Powered Development Stack
session.description:
Most teams adopt AI coding assistants and discover the same thing within a month. The output is fast, the output is plausible, the output is wrong in ways that take longer to fix than writing it yourself. The gap isn't the model. It's everything around the model. This talk is about what fills that gap.
We'll walk through the architecture of a production AI engineering system, drawn from our experience building, shipping, and rebuilding our own at Very Good Ventures. The frame is layered. At the bottom, contextual knowledge that teaches the AI your standards. In the middle, structured workflows that replace ad-hoc prompting with brainstorm, plan, and build phases. At the top, guardrails through hooks, MCP integrations, and automated analysis that catch problems before they reach a PR. Each layer answers a different failure mode, and skipping any of them is where most teams stall.
The talk covers the design decisions that actually mattered. Why we moved from monolithic prompts to discrete skills. Why brainstorming and planning needed to be separate phases instead of one step. What we got wrong about tool-agnosticism the first time, and what changed when we tried again. How to know when a convention belongs in AI context versus a linter versus a code review checklist. The patterns generalize beyond Flutter, but we'll ground every principle in real examples from Flutter work so the lessons are concrete, not abstract.
You'll leave with a model for how the pieces fit together, a clearer sense of which layer to invest in first for your team, and a few opinionated takes on what to avoid.

Key Learnings
- A layered model for AI-assisted development, contextual knowledge, structured workflows, and automated guardrails, and why each layer matters
- How to decompose a feature into brainstorm, plan, and build phases that produce better output than direct prompting
- How to encode team standards as contextual skills the AI actually reaches for, versus standards that get ignored
- Where hooks and MCP integrations belong in the loop, and where they add friction without value
- Practical guidance for sequencing adoption so quality and velocity move together instead of trading off

session.title: Lunch
The Flutter Enterprise Journey: From Native Apps to AI-Enabled Multi-Platform
session.description:
This session explores how enterprise teams are navigating the strategic shift from native mobile development to Flutter's unified multi-platform approach, examining the real-world challenges, trade-offs, and wins along the way. We'll dive into why Flutter is uniquely positioned as an AI-era framework - where a single shared codebase dramatically reduces the token overhead of AI-assisted development, meaning fewer redundant prompts, less repeated logic across platforms, and faster iteration cycles when working alongside tools like GitHub Copilot or Claude.

Attendees will walk away with a practical roadmap for modernizing their enterprise app strategy with AI at the center, unlocking compounding efficiency gains across iOS, Android, web, and desktop without sacrificing the performance, security, and maintainability that production environments demand.

session.title: Next steps and new patterns for GenUI
session.description: By now, you've likely heard about Generative UI, a UX architecture in which an agent not only generates content, but also reasons about how that content should be displayed and made interactive. In this talk, we'll go a step further than introductions and talk about more advanced topics like caching A2UI messages, combining local and agentic state, and more!

session.title: Designer + Engineer + Claude: Building a Cross-Functional AI Workflow from Figma to Flutter
session.description:
Elevator pitch: The handoff between design and engineering is where most Flutter products lose fidelity, time, and trust. We'll show how a VGV product designer and engineer used Claude end-to-end on a real client engagement by merging two native design systems into one AI-readable Figma system, then generating theme-integrated, golden-tested Flutter widgets that match design intent pixel-for-pixel.

Description (~250 words):
Most "AI in Flutter" talks are told from one side of the wall: an engineer using a model to write code faster. The interesting workflow lives on both sides of that wall, and across it.In this session, a VGV lead product designer and engineering lead walk through a real client engagement where Claude became a shared collaborator across the full design-to-code pipeline.
  • Refining the design system for AI readability: Before a line of code was written, we used Claude to interrogate, restructure, and improve an existing Figma system. Think token naming, component conventions, variant structure, documentation. Not just for human clarity, but for machine readability. This included establishing AI readability best practices that made downstream code generation dramatically more reliable.
  • Claude Code inside Figma: Using Claude Code via the Figma Desktop Bridge MCP, allowed us to accelerate the design system refinement itself. By enabling this, Claude had the ability to directly edit inside Figma in real time: bulk renaming, correcting inconsistencies, writing documentation, creating variables. Fast, precise, and collaborative.
  • Generating the specs: Turning Figma artifacts into structured, machine-readable specifications engineering can actually consume.
  • Implementing in Flutter: A Figma-to-Flutter agent skill that reads design tokens directly via the Figma MCP server, generates theme-integrated widgets, and validates pixel fidelity with LLM driven visual comparison, and Widgetbook use cases for human validation.
  • VGV good practices: Feature-First Clean Architecture, Widgetbook, Very Good CLI scaffolding, and the guardrails that let AI accelerate without eroding quality.You'll leave with a concrete blueprint for a cross-functional AI workflow (not a demo reel), honest lessons from what works and what doesn't yet, and a clear sense of why pairing a product designer and engineer on AI tooling produces something neither gets to alone. Built on a live client engagement, presented by the people who actually shipped it.

What attendees will learn
  • A repeatable pattern for using AI in the design system definition phase, before engineering starts.
  • How to structure Figma so an agent can consume it reliably, including token, component, and variant conventions.
  • The mechanics of a Figma-to-Flutter skill: MCP integration, code generation, theme integration, golden-test validation.
  • Where the human stays in the loop on both the design and engineering sides, and why that matters.
  • How this fits into Feature-First Clean Architecture and the wider VGV agentic engineering practice.

session.title: Self-Healing Flutter UI with Widgetbook: Teaching Coding Agents to Judge Their Own Work
session.description:
Coding agents generate Flutter UI fast. The hard problem isn't speed anymore, it's quality. Agents ship pull requests where the code compiles, the tests pass, and the widget looks right at first glance. But the design system has been quietly violated. The layout breaks on a small phone nobody tested. The accessibility label exists but doesn't actually help a screen reader user. Reviewers don't have time to catch all of it. UI debt accumulates at twice the speed.
This talk shows a workflow that flips the problem. The agent verifies its own work before a human ever sees the PR. We use Flutter's structured trees (the Widget Tree and the Semantics Tree) as ground truth. Widgetbook turns those trees into compact, actionable diffs that an agent can read, reason about, and fix in a tight loop. Then, and only then, does the human get a clean, summarized PR to review. Code changes can be reviewed in Git while UI changes can be reviewed in Widgetbook Cloud.

Widgetbook's customer Couchsurfing will join the stage to showcase how they have been using Widgetbook.

Walk away with a complete UI agent-assisted development for Flutter: how to give your design system to an agent as context, how to build self-healing test loops with flutter test, and how to review your agents work.

session.title: Your Next Flutter Teammate Is Not Human
session.description:
AI is rapidly becoming part of the Flutter workflow, but not in the way it is usually presented in conference talks. This is not about adding a chatbot to your app; it is about a deeper shift: AI is starting to behave like a teammate inside the development loop.

In this roundtable, we will explore what happens when prompt-based tools step into tasks like UI scaffolding, repetitive coding, test generation, refactoring, documentation, and debugging. We will compare real experiences: where these tools genuinely save time, where they introduce risks or false confidence, and how blindly accepting generated code can actually degrade a codebase instead of improving it.

The goal is not to promote AI hype or reject it, but to build a more grounded, shared perspective: how can Flutter teams work with these tools while maintaining speed, critical thinking, and strong engineering judgment?
