session.title: Break
Leading Flutter at Scale: From Feature Velocity to Platform Thinking
session.description:
When Flutter starts as a fast-moving feature engine, velocity is everything. But as adoption grows and the app becomes business-critical, the constraints shift. Architecture decisions become organizational decisions. Platform choices impact hiring, delivery, and long-term maintainability. What once optimized for speed must now optimize for durability.

In this session, I’ll share lessons from leading Flutter teams as they evolved from feature-focused development to platform-oriented thinking. We’ll explore how architectural decisions mature alongside team structure, how to avoid accidental complexity as codebases grow, and how to balance innovation with operational stability.

This talk is not about widgets or state management patterns. It’s about leadership decisions that determine whether Flutter remains an accelerator, or becomes a liability, at scale.

Key Takeaways

- How to recognize when your Flutter app has become a platform, not just a product
- Architectural patterns that scale across teams and years, not just sprints
- Organizational decisions that impact long-term technical health
- Common failure points when Flutter adoption expands
- Balancing feature velocity with architectural integrity

This session is grounded in real-world experience building and operating large, production Flutter systems with cross-functional teams and evolving business demands.

session.title: Dependency Injection in Enterprise Flutter Applications
session.description:
Dependency injection (DI) is a crucial pattern for ensuring application maintainability and testability. These aspects are especially important in enterprise applications, where DI can become significantly more complex to implement effectively. In modular applications developed by multiple teams, DI requires additional consideration to ensure loose coupling and clear dependency definitions while avoiding cumbersome boilerplate.

In this talk, we will explore:
- DI challenges in enterprise Flutter applications
- DI solutions used in a couple of enterprise Flutter projects
- The benefits and drawbacks of each approach

session.title: LEGO Architecture for Enterprise-Scale Flutter Applications
session.description:
As mobile applications grow into large, long-lived systems, maintaining a clear and scalable codebase becomes increasingly challenging. This talk explores a production-proven architectural approach for large-scale Flutter applications, successfully used for several years in a product with millions of users and a multi-million-line codebase, developed by a team of 100+ mobile engineers.

Built around a “LEGO” concept, where the application is composed of highly modular, interchangeable, self-contained building blocks, this architecture enables teams to develop and deliver features in isolation, while keeping the overall system consistent and maintainable. We’ll cover key aspects such as code organization, state management, navigation, dependency injection, configuration, testing, and more, along with the principles that guided these choices. 

You’ll leave with practical recommendations for structuring large codebases and scaling development without sacrificing efficiency or maintainability.

session.title: Unleashing On-Device AI with LiteRT and Flutter
session.description: Discover how to supercharge your Flutter apps with on-device AI using the new LiteRT. This talk explores deploying on-device AI models seamlessly multi-platform, i.e. Gemma, Qwen, and classical models. Learn to leverage native GPU and NPU acceleration to deliver lightning-fast, intelligent experiences without compromising performance.

session.title: Gemma 4 on Device: Roundtable on Real-World Gotchas, Wins, and What's Next
session.description:
Gemma 4 dropped in April 2026, and the on-device Flutter story is genuinely exciting: streaming inference, native function calling, thinking mode, multimodal input, all running locally, with zero API costs and no data leaving the device. The flutter_gemma package makes it surprisingly approachable. And then you actually build something.

This roundtable gathers Flutter developers currently working with or evaluating Gemma 4 and the flutter_gemma package. I will begin our conversation by sharing practical insights from the recent launch of Lumina Journal, an AI-powered journaling application that operates entirely on-device, powered by Gemma 4 E2B.

Key topics for discussion include:
  * Technical challenges, such as silent GPU fallback on mid-range Android devices.
  * Performance metrics for function-calling reliability and thinking-mode token budgets.
  * User experience hurdles, such as the significant 2.5 GB initial download requirement.

While documentation provides a baseline, the actual implementation often reveals unexpected complexities. This session is a collaborative forum to exchange lessons learned, address unanswered questions, and define the current state and future trajectory of on-device AI within the Flutter ecosystem as of 2026.

session.title: Order Up: High-Stakes Leadership Lessons from 20 Years Behind the Bar
session.description:
What does a Friday night rush at a packed bar have in common with a production outage? More than you’d think. In an era where AI can handle the syntax, the "human" architecture of engineering—de-escalation, reading the room, and managing chaos—has become the ultimate senior-level differentiator. Drawing on two decades in hospitality and a career in software leadership, this session translates "bar logic" into a tactical framework for leading high-performing Flutter teams.

We’ll explore how to apply "Mise en Place" to your development workflows and use "Service Recovery" to turn failed deployments into loyalty-building moments. Whether you’re a solo founder or a team lead, you’ll learn why the most resilient leaders don’t just write code—they "manage the floor." Leave the IDE behind and discover how hospitality-honed intuition can help you navigate the messy, non-binary reality of human collaboration.

session.title: Break
Multi-Platform Strategic Advantage @ Scale: Flutter at UDX and Sky
session.description:
Sky and Universal Destinations & Experiences are both part of  Comcast Corporation. Both have pushed Flutter far past mobile/web. Universal Destinations & Experiences (UDX) runs Flutter across the official park app and across the in-park kiosks deployed throughout its theme parks. At Sky, Flutter will run across consumer apps and across Entertainment OS, the TV platform serving millions of households.

Engineering leaders from Sky and Universal will headline this panel to discuss why multi-platform reach is no longer a nice-to-have for entertainment brands. It is the strategic advantage. The conversation will cover what changes when a single framework spans mobile apps, in-park hardware, and a TV/STB operating system. How distinct branded experiences ship from a shared codebase, even when the brand promise shifts surface to surface. And how standardizing on Flutter reshapes design organizations, product decision-making, and the business case for digital investment inside large entertainment enterprises.

If you lead a Flutter team or own digital product at consumer scale, this is the closest look you will get at two of the most ambitious Flutter deployments in the world.

session.title: Type-Safe Isolate Communication in Flutter with Code Generation
session.description:
Working with isolates in Flutter can quickly become complex, especially when passing messages and maintaining type safety. In this session, I’ll walk through how I built a code generator that simplifies communication across isolates by automatically generating strongly-typed stubs for classes running on separate isolates.

This talk is also a practical deep dive into building a Dart code generator—from understanding the underlying tooling to designing an API that feels seamless for developers. I’ll share lessons learned from forking and extending an existing package, and how I evolved it into a more ergonomic solution.

We plan to open-source this generator after the talk to share the approach and gather feedback from the community.

Key Takeaways:

Why isolate communication becomes a bottleneck in real-world Flutter apps (and how it forced us into a temporary “merged isolate” workaround)
How to design and build a Dart code generator using source_gen/build_runner
Techniques to preserve type safety across isolate boundaries
How to redesign architecture to properly separate multiple isolates for better CPU and memory efficiency
Lessons learned from extending and improving an open-source package

session.title: Render the Contract: A boundary architecture for generated UIs in Flutter
session.description:
Flutter made UI feel simple to reason about: compose widgets, connect state, render the app. Generated UI changes that model. As interfaces become more dynamic and generated on demand, the question shifts from “how do I build this screen?” to “what can external systems ask my app to show, bind, and do?”

This session walks through a boundary architecture for generated UI in Flutter. The contract is more than a component schema: it defines the vocabulary of app surfaces, state bindings, action intents, interaction flows, and capabilities that external systems can use.

We’ll follow how a request from a server, schema, agent, or tool becomes a resolved render model and then ordinary Flutter widgets. You’ll leave with a practical model for keeping state, actions, and behavior app-owned while still letting external systems request UI on demand.

A schema asks. An agent asks. A tool asks. The contract decides. Flutter renders.

session.title: Voice Interfaces in Flutter: Building Assistants with Speech-to-Text and Text-to-Speech
session.description:
Voice is becoming one of the most natural ways to interact with technology, and Flutter gives us the tools to bring it into our apps today. In this session, we’ll explore how to build a voice-enabled assistant in Flutter by combining Speech-to-Text (STT) for recognition and Text-to-Speech (TTS) for natural responses. We’ll cover the available Flutter plugins and APIs, compare cloud vs. offline solutions, and highlight pitfalls like dealing with Austrian dialect.
Through live demos, you’ll see how to integrate STT and TTS into a Flutter app, structure your architecture for responsive voice interactions, and design UIs that feel intuitive when voice becomes the primary input. By the end of the talk, you’ll have the knowledge to extend your Flutter apps with conversational capabilities—whether for accessibility, productivity, or full-blown voice assistants.

session.title: HUMAN.md: Are we losing our skills in the SKILL.md era?
session.description: Google’s SKILL.md standard teaches agents to navigate our codebases, but our own human skills are throwing a NullPointerException. Historically, the Android developer ladder was built on "productive friction"—debugging crashes, arguing architecture in PRs, and obsessing over UI jank. AI removes this friction, causing a human-skill atrophy. Juniors now prompt stack traces instead of learning to debug. Mid-levels rely on AI scaffolding, completely missing the architectural "blast radius" intuition. And seniors are mutating from master builders into full-time hallucination auditors. This talk breaks down the ledger of what we’ve gained versus what we’ve lost, and how to intentionally inject "synthetic friction" back into our workflows to prevent our engineering teams from becoming technically hollow.

session.title: Lunch
Flutter Beyond Mobile: Lessons from Bringing Flutter to Embedded Hardware
session.description:
Flutter's promise of true multiplatform goes beyond mobile, web, and desktop. In this session, Aaron Crutchfield (NuStep) and Kyle Crouse (Very Good Ventures) share an engineering case study on bringing Flutter to embedded fitness hardware and the realities of running Flutter in environments it wasn't originally designed for.

Key takeaways:
- How to architect a Flutter app for embedded Linux
- Where Flutter's multiplatform story genuinely delivers
- A real-world collaboration model between a hardware company and a Flutter agency

session.title: Flutteristas Panel: Is Your App Inclusive, or Does It Just Check the Boxes?
session.description: For years, "inclusivity" in mobile development has been treated as a synonym for standard accessibility compliance or simply adding a third option to a gender dropdown. But true inclusion runs much deeper—it’s baked into our database schemas, our form validations, and our UX assumptions. Back for their third year at FlutterCon USA, the Flutteristas are diving into the hidden nuances of identity and inclusion in tech. From the pitfalls of rigid form design to the unintentional exclusion in niche markets like parenting and tracking apps, this panel will explore how "inclusivity in the app" doesn’t always match real-world lived experiences. Join us for a practical, honest conversation on how to evaluate your apps from fresh perspectives, re-architect your data structures, and build software that genuinely represents the diverse world we live in.

session.title: Auth flows in Flutter WebViews without losing your mind (or your users' sessions)
session.description:
Every hybrid Flutter app eventually hits the same wall: the user logs in through a WebView, closes it, and the native side has no idea who they are. Cookies vanish on iOS. Redirects fire twice on Android. SSO works in dev and breaks in TestFlight. Session refresh quietly logs people out at 3am.

Auth in WebViews is one of the least-documented and most-shipped patterns in Flutter, and getting it wrong costs you retention. This talk walks through the full lifecycle — launching the WebView, intercepting redirects, sharing cookies with your native HTTP client, persisting sessions across app restarts, and handling expiry gracefully. You'll leave with a reference architecture, a checklist of the platform gotchas that will bite you, and working code for the two or three patterns that actually hold up in production.

session.title: Where Dash meets Tux - The Flutter Linux Embedder
session.description:
The Flutter Linux Embedder allows Flutter apps to run on the Linux platform. This session will describe:
 * How the Linux platform works and differs from other platforms like Windows and macOS.
 * Describe how the Linux embedder was developed and some of the technical decisions that were made.
 * Discuss other options in Linux and how the embedder might evolve over time.

If you want to know more about how your Flutter application interfaces with Linux or are interested in developing your own embedder, this session is for you!

session.title: Break
Escape Hatches at Scale: How Nubank Keeps Flutter Fluid Inside a Heavy Stack
session.description:
Nubank’s Flutter app serves more than 125 million customers across Brazil, Mexico, and Colombia. At that scale, consistent delivery requires canonical approaches: Nu Design System standardizes the UI, while Backend Driven Content enables hundreds of teams to ship experiences through a single multi-country application.

But production delivery and exploration optimize for different things. The canonical path provides safety, consistency, and governance; exploration needs variation, autonomy, and a low cost of being wrong. Nubank’s Concept Car was created as a structured, non-production Flutter environment where designers and engineers can stress-test motion, transitions, loading states, haptics, sound, and the boundaries of Flutter itself — without bypassing the discipline of the production ecosystem.

AI is now changing who can participate in that process. Designers can move from design intent toward runnable Flutter experiments more independently, shortening the distance between an idea and something that can be experienced. But once creating experiments becomes faster, distribution becomes the next bottleneck: how can another designer or stakeholder open the result on a real device without waiting for packaging, CI/CD, or traditional app distribution?

In this joint talk, Nubank and LeanCode share how Concept Car provides the environment, AI gives designers greater agency, and Flitz gives experiments reach. We’ll show how Flutter preview environments can deliver portable bytecode bundles to real devices in minutes, while keeping the production release pipeline intact — and discuss the trade-offs of building a fast feedback lane alongside a mobile platform operating at massive scale.

session.title: Flutter Scene: Add 3D to your Flutter apps!
session.description:
Did you know that Flutter supports building arbitrary renderers in Dart?
Today, you can render gorgeous 3D in your Flutter apps using the Flutter Scene package, powered by the Flutter GPU API!

From maps to emojis to games, we'll explore the fun and practical use cases of 3D apps built with Flutter.

session.title: Best Practices, Pitfalls, and the future - A look at production-ready SDUI
session.description:
- Take a peek at how Headspace is building reactive, experimentation-ready interfaces without having to submit new app versions
- Learn some of the issues that we found along the way, and how we fix them
- Learn about some of the gaps we found in production environments that you may want to consider when building for SDUI

session.title: How Flutter Stays Ahead of iOS Releases
session.description:
Every June, Apple drops a massive wave of updates at WWDC that can redefine the iOS and macOS ecosystems. Because Flutter runs natively on Apple devices, our team’s top priority is ensuring that Flutter works well on every new iOS release. That means staying on top of the latest Apple features, adopting new platform capabilities, and catching breaking changes in Xcode betas before they hit your screen. For the Flutter iOS team, this kicks off a comprehensive, fast-paced triage effort. 

Fresh off the back of WWDC26, I’ll show you guys how we actually digest it all. We'll look at how we summarize dozens of sessions, read between the lines of Apple's new documentation, and translate everything into a neatly prioritized backlog of GitHub issues ready to be worked on by anyone.

Key Takeaways: 
-The pipeline the Flutter team uses to watch, digest, and log WWDC updates 

-How we separate critical, breaking, P0 changes and deprecations from flashy, nice-to-have features when prioritizing work.

-How we use WWDC to look at our existing backlog and decide what we can safely keep ignoring versus what NEEDS to be fixed right now.

session.title: The Future of Digital is Physical
session.description:
Screens are everywhere, so why does it feel like nobody wants them around? Vinyl records, film cameras, and paper planners are selling at record rates. The pendulum has swung: in a world drowning in pixels, physical is the new novelty.

But what if the answer isn't less technology... It's technology that blends seamlessly into the real world?

Pull up a chair with Matthew "Makerinator" Jones for an open conversation about physical-digital hybrid experiences: the philosophy powering them, the cultural moments demanding them, and where Flutter and Dart fit in a toolkit that increasingly includes microcontrollers, sensors, and 3D printers. 

Bring your projects, your skepticism, and your half-baked ideas! We'll trade notes on what's working, what isn't, and what's worth trying next.

Oh, and a fortune-telling fish named Captain Reef might wander by. She sees the future and has opinions.

session.title: Portable Hooks: Enforcing Engineering Tenets Across Claude Code, Codex, Antigravity, and OpenCode
session.description:
In this talk, I'm going to talk about how I've been setting up my projects for a future where most of the code is written by agents. It's coming, and the sooner you prepare for it, the better.

For many years, we've enforced architecture, best practices, and formatting on our code, but only parts of it were ever mechanically enforced. The majority were conventions we could apply with common sense. Code review was easier too, because we weren't getting hundreds of lines of code a week to review.

Nowadays, every coding agent harness ships with some kind of lifecycle hook: a script that lets you run code before or after each operation the model does. So before any write or edit to a file in your codebase, you can intercept that command and inspect it. You can see exactly what the model is trying to write, and compare it against your best practices, your architecture, your conventions, and so on.

If you've used coding agents to write Android code, you've probably seen them putting business logic into composables, not respecting unidirectional flow and view model responsibilities, using managers, or creating patterns that don't fit your codebase, like event buses or Context inside the business logic. It's a mess.

All of these can be avoided with a lifecycle hook that identifies the bad practice, intercepts it before it happens, and tells the agent what to do instead. The bad code never even gets written into a file.

On top of that, some hooks can weigh in on the decisions the agent is making. You can nudge it when it makes a decision that goes against your tenets, when it decides to stop working, or when it violates one of your directives. For example: "don't stop until you're fully done, and verify that your acceptance criteria are actually implemented."

You can run a script that checks the backlog the model's been keeping track of and nudges it to keep going if it isn't done.

There's much, much more I'll show and talk through.

At the end, I'm going to show you how you can easily bring a whole set of these hooks into your app, with an open-source project I'm excited to finally release. I've been working on it for a few months now and using it on my own projects, and we've implemented it at Realtor, where we're happy to say we're slop-free in our codebase!

It installs hooks for any agentic coding system with just a few commands, and you get all the best practices: you select the ones you want, or select everything, and bring it into your codebase.

Come join me in this talk and learn how to prepare your codebase for modern agentic coding and workflows.

session.title: Break
Flutter @ DraftKings - Animating the Game
session.description:
Building a Real-Time Sports Tracker with Flame, Rive & Flutter
and turns raw real-time sports data into an animated, interactive, embeddable game visualization

session.title: Build Once, Ship Everywhere: Enterprise CI/CD for Flutter Monorepos
session.description: We migrated a large native iOS and Android app to a Flutter monorepo and built the CI/CD infrastructure from scratch — GitHub Actions for CI, Codemagic for CD, and Melos holding the monorepo together. This talk covers the architecture decisions that made the pipeline fast and reliable: path-based triggers that only run what changed, parallelized test execution across four threads with randomized ordering, 100% coverage enforcement as a gate (not a goal), and ephemeral build environments with smart caching that keep things reproducible without being slow. We'll walk through how a five-layer package structure with strict dependency rules enables per-package workflows that scale. You'll leave with a practical framework for building Flutter monorepo CI/CD that gives your team fast feedback loops, clean environments, and confidence that what passes CI is ready to ship.

session.title: What Blind Users Taught Me About Building Better Flutter Apps
session.description:
This talk covers practical accessibility lessons I learned after my Flutter app unexpectedly caught on with blind users. I'll share what I got wrong, what actually matters for VoiceOver support, and how to build accessibility in from the start.

Key takeaways:

1. Adding semantic labels is just the beginning. Real VoiceOver support requires rethinking navigation, focus order, and how state changes are announced.

2. I'll cover practical setup and use of accessibility features for visually impaired users, including VoiceOver, rotor actions, and dark mode support.

3. Direct user feedback reveals friction points you'd never catch on your own.

4. Small fixes often have outsized impact on usability.

5. Building for accessibility makes your app better for everyone.

session.title: GenUI, Powered by Google's ADK
session.description:
GenUI enables an LLM to determine an interface at runtime, but current practice puts all of the responsibility on the client. This comes with many drawbacks, including exposing access keys and network traffic and adding latency.

Using Google's Agent Development Kit (ADK), we can move (almost) all of that responsibility back onto the server, minimizing exposure and network calls, while keeping our Flutter apps lightweight and secure. As a bonus, we gain access to the ADK's powerful tool, agent, and workflow composition APIs.

Flutter is great at rendering beautiful interfaces and GenUI enables unique, personalized experiences. Google's ADK is what powers enterprise-grade agents at scale.

session.title: Break
Building Flutter at Scale: Real-World Monorepo Practices from QuintoAndar
session.description:
What does it take to run a massive Flutter codebase with 60+ engineers, 100+ modules, and multiple apps—mobile and web?

At QuintoAndar, we've spent the last couple of years scaling our Flutter monorepo to meet the demands of a fast-growing, multi-platform product suite. This talk dives deep into the real-world challenges we've faced, architectural decisions, and tooling we use to keep our developers happy and productive.

We’ll cover how we organize and integrate hundreds of modular packages, sharing code between multiple of our products in format of Flutter mobile and web apps, while also improving the developer experience for both seasoned engineers and newcomers.

session.title: Spec Your Vibe with TDD
session.description:
Agentic coding is a transformative new reality. Human engineers must provide coding rules, workflow descriptions, and a keen eye to make sure our agents code at our level or even better.

It’s also important, however, to be smart about how we add new features to our apps. Piling more specifications on top of an already bloated agent context is likely to result in costly hallucinations.

Come to this talk to learn how to create simple and self-updating behavioral specs using existing Test Driven Development (TDD) techniques, including Gherkin syntax and Snapshot testing. We’ll wrap it all up with a demonstration of this process using OpenSpec - THE emerging industry standard for Spec Driven Development!

Vibe smart, not hard!

session.title: Beyond Static Widgets: Dynamic Flutter UI for Regulated and Generative Use Cases
session.description: This session explores how to dynamically render Flutter widgets to power two very different kinds of experiences: deterministic, server-driven UI for regulated environments where compliance and predictability are non-negotiable, and non-deterministic, AI-driven UI generated on the fly using LLMs and the Model Context Protocol (MCP). We'll look at the architectural patterns, trade-offs, and guardrails that make both approaches viable in production.

session.title: The Swarm Next Door: Running Parallel AI Agents on a Real Android Codebase
session.description:
I work on a large Android codebase — the kind with long Gradle builds, stacked PRs, and more context switches than I can keep in my head. Last year I started using AI coding agents. They were helpful. They were
also constantly in my way, because they lived in the same checkout I was trying to work in.

So I started giving each agent its own git worktree and its own tmux pane. Isolated filesystem, visible output, killable with one keystroke. That solved the interference problem. Then I started running two
agents at once. Then three. Now I'm experimenting with agent swarms — orchestrating sub-agents that decompose tasks and farm them out in parallel, each in its own worktree, reporting back to a coordinator.

This talk is an honest look at where that workflow is right now. I'll show what actually works: parallel test generation, mechanical refactoring, and documentation across branches you're not even looking at.
I'll show what doesn't: entangled features, anything requiring real architectural judgment, and the subtle ways agents break things when you stop watching. I'll demo the full setup live — tmux, worktrees, agents
running in parallel on a multi-module Android project — including the Gradle cache tricks that make it survivable.

Research says experienced developers using AI tools actually work 19% slower than they think they do. I believe it. This talk is about building a workflow where you can verify that it's helping, and drop it when
it's not.

session.title: End of Show
